metadata.subject: Social Studies
metadata.description: Contemporary Indigenous Studies 12 focuses on Indigenous identities, worldviews, resilience, and self-determination in Canada and globally. Students engage with Indigenous ways of knowing, examine the ongoing effects of colonialism, and explore paths toward reconciliation. The course centres Indigenous voices, scholarship, and community perspectives as its primary sources.
metadata.general_description: A contemporary study of Indigenous peoples in Canada and globally, examining identity, worldview, colonialism's ongoing impacts, rights advocacy, and the journey toward reconciliation and self-determination.
metadata.course_notes: This course is designed with Indigenous perspectives at its centre. Engagement with local Elders, knowledge keepers, and Indigenous-led scholarship is strongly encouraged.

big_ideas.statement: The identities, worldviews, and languages of indigenous peoples are renewed, sustained, and transformed through their connection to the land.
big_ideas.elaboration: Land is the foundation of Indigenous identity — it shapes language, ceremony, governance, and spiritual life, and its loss is an ongoing source of harm.

big_ideas.statement: Indigenous peoples are reclaiming mental, emotional, physical, and spiritual well-being despite the continuing effects of colonialism.
big_ideas.elaboration: Through cultural revitalization, healing practices, and community development, Indigenous peoples are actively working to recover from colonial trauma.

big_ideas.statement: Indigenous peoples continue to advocate and assert rights to self-determination.
big_ideas.elaboration: In courtrooms, legislatures, protests, and communities, Indigenous peoples are asserting their right to govern themselves and control their futures.

big_ideas.statement: Reconciliation requires all colonial societies to work together to foster healing and address injustices.
big_ideas.elaboration: Reconciliation is not just an Indigenous responsibility — it requires non-Indigenous individuals, governments, and institutions to acknowledge and address historical and ongoing injustices.

categories.name: Inquiry with Indigenous Principles
categories.raw: Use Social Studies inquiry processes and skills to ask questions; listen to the oral tradition of Elders. Use indigenous principles of learning (holistic, experiential, reflective, and relational) to better understand connectedness.

competencies.raw: Use Social Studies inquiry processes and Indigenous principles of learning to investigate issues and communicate findings.
i_can_statements: I can form inquiry questions that are respectful of Indigenous knowledge and community protocols.
I can listen to and learn from Elders and knowledge keepers using appropriate cultural protocols.
I can apply Indigenous learning principles (holistic, experiential, reflective, relational) in my research.
I can present findings in ways that are accurate and respectful to Indigenous communities.

categories.name: Significance
categories.raw: Assess and compare the significance of the interconnections between people, places, events.

competencies.raw: Assess and compare the significance of interconnections between people, places, and events in Indigenous contexts.
i_can_statements: I can explain the significance of a specific treaty, court case, or movement for Indigenous self-determination.
I can compare how the same event is viewed from Indigenous and settler perspectives.
I can evaluate the significance of land in Indigenous worldviews compared to Western legal frameworks.

categories.name: Evidence
categories.raw: Ask questions and corroborate inferences of Elders and other local knowledge keepers.

competencies.raw: Ask questions and corroborate inferences using Indigenous knowledge and a variety of sources.
i_can_statements: I can analyze oral tradition as a credible historical and legal source.
I can corroborate Indigenous oral history with other forms of evidence.
I can evaluate academic and government sources on Indigenous issues for bias and accuracy.
I can identify whose knowledge is centred and whose is marginalized in a source.

categories.name: Continuity and Change
categories.raw: Compare and contrast continuities and changes for different groups in different time periods and places.

competencies.raw: Compare and contrast continuities and changes for Indigenous groups across time and place.
i_can_statements: I can trace how the legal status of Indigenous peoples in Canada has changed over time.
I can compare the contemporary situation of Indigenous peoples in Canada with those in other colonial nations.
I can identify what aspects of colonial policy persist today in different forms.

categories.name: Cause and Consequence
categories.raw: Assess how underlying conditions and the actions of individuals or groups affect events.

competencies.raw: Assess how underlying conditions and actions affect contemporary Indigenous issues.
i_can_statements: I can trace the connection between historical colonial policies and contemporary Indigenous inequities.
I can identify the factors that drive Indigenous land rights movements.
I can evaluate the intended and unintended consequences of reconciliation policies.

categories.name: Perspective
categories.raw: Explain different perspectives on past or present people, places, issues, and events.

competencies.raw: Explain different perspectives on Indigenous issues from Indigenous and non-Indigenous viewpoints.
i_can_statements: I can explain how Indigenous and non-Indigenous people may interpret the same law or event differently.
I can represent diverse Indigenous perspectives without treating Indigenous peoples as a monolith.
I can analyze how media representations of Indigenous peoples shape public opinion.

categories.name: Ethical Judgment
categories.raw: Make reasoned ethical claims about actions in the past and present.

competencies.raw: Make reasoned ethical claims about actions in the past and present related to Indigenous peoples.
i_can_statements: I can make an ethical argument about what reconciliation requires of non-Indigenous Canadians.
I can evaluate whether Canada has met its legal and moral obligations to Indigenous peoples.
I can discuss what meaningful Indigenous consent looks like in resource development.

areas.name: Varied Identities, Worldviews, Languages, and Land Connections
areas.raw: Varied identities, worldviews, languages, and land connections.
topics: The diversity of Indigenous peoples globally: nations, languages, and cultures
Indigenous worldviews: relational, land-based, and holistic perspectives
Language revitalization: the role of language in cultural identity
Land as identity: different Indigenous nations' relationships to specific territories
The urban Indigenous experience: identity outside traditional territories

areas.name: Factors Sustaining and Challenging Indigenous Identities
areas.raw: Factors sustaining and challenging Indigenous identities.
topics: Cultural revitalization: language nests, drumming circles, land-based education
Intergenerational trauma and its impact on identity
The role of ceremony, storytelling, and art in sustaining identity
Challenges to identity: racism, stereotyping, systemic marginalization
The intersection of urban life and Indigenous identity

areas.name: Resilience and Survival Under Colonialism
areas.raw: Resilience and survival responses to colonialism.
topics: Forms of Indigenous resistance: political, legal, cultural, and spiritual
Healing and wellness: reclaiming mental, physical, emotional, and spiritual health
Intergenerational transmission of resilience and cultural knowledge
Indigenous literature, film, and art as expressions of survivance
Case studies in Indigenous community resilience

areas.name: Community Development and Economic Opportunities
topics: Indigenous economic development: band-owned businesses, Indigenous tourism, resource partnerships
The tension between resource development and land stewardship
Indigenous education systems: from residential schools to Indigenous-controlled schools
Healthcare in Indigenous communities: challenges and Indigenous healing approaches
Housing and infrastructure on and off reserves

areas.name: Responses to Inequities with Governments
topics: The legal framework for Indigenous rights: Section 35, UNDRIP, modern treaties
The MMIWG National Inquiry: findings and calls for justice
Indigenous responses to resource extraction projects (pipelines, mines, dams)
Idle No More and other 21st century Indigenous movements
Working within and outside government systems for change

areas.name: Truth, Healing, and Reconciliation
topics: The Truth and Reconciliation Commission: process, findings, and 94 Calls to Action
What reconciliation means: definitions, frameworks, and critiques
The role of non-Indigenous Canadians in reconciliation
Orange Shirt Day and public acknowledgment of residential school harm
International examples of reconciliation: South Africa, Australia, New Zealand
Progress and setbacks on the Calls to Action
